retrieved_docs.title: Bispecific Antibody Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Blinatumomab (2014): For CD19-positive B-cell precursor acute lymphoblastic leukemia therapy Emicizumab (2017): For reducing bleeding frequency in Hemophilia A with factor VIII inhibitors Amivantamab (2021): For treating locally advanced or metastatic non–small cell lung cancer with epidermal growth factor receptor exon-20 insertion mutation Faricimab (2022): For therapy of neovascular age-related macular degeneration and diabetic macular edema Teclistamab (2022): For relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma treatment Mosunetuzumab (2022): For relapsed or refractory follicular lymphoma therapy Epcoritamab and glofitamab (2023):  For treating relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma Talquetamab and elranatamab (2023): For therapy of relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanisms of action associated with mAbs include direct cell toxicity, immune-mediated cell toxicity, vascular disruption, and modulation of the immune system. Their evolution has placed mAbs at the forefront of highly individualized therapeutics for a variety of cancers and autoimmune diseases whilst limiting the adverse systemic effects associated with traditional treatment options.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Yet, despite these advancements and the wide variety of mAbs now available on the market, there is still significant work to be done to identify novel mAb targets and optimize the pharmacology-related properties of such molecules. Future directions include the continued development and refinement of bispecific antibodies, which have the potential to target multiple therapeutic targets, activate multiple pathways simultaneously, and enhance the potency of therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Unfortunately, the pathophysiology for many of the conditions in which mAbs are applied tends to be complex and not mediated by a single mechanism or molecule. [11] [12] A potential solution to this dilemma is the advent of bispecific mAbs (bsmAbs), whereby the concept of targeting multiple pathophysiological mechanisms may improve the efficacy of therapy. [11] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: In 2022, the FDA approved teclistamab, the first bispecific T-cell engager antibody against CD38 expressed on the myeloma cells, for patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who have failed 4 prior lines of therapy. Belantamab mafodotin is anti-BCMA humanized immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody conjugated with monomethyl auristatin. The drug received accelerated approval in the US for patients who had progressed on at least 4 lines of therapy. It is being withdrawn from the market based on the results of randomized phase 3 trials (DREAMM-3), as it did not show clinical benefit compared to pomalidomide and dexamethasone.

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the initial challenges associated with murine methodologies of mAb development, research continued, and the development of alternative methods overcame these limitations. Chimeric clones were the next developments, whereby human crystallizable fragment (Fc) regions were attached in place of murine ones. [5] Examples of chimeric mAbs include infliximab and rituximab. [1] Chimeric clones were followed by developing "humanization," a process where murine protein loops (which served as ligand binding sites) were implanted within human immunoglobulins. [4] [5] Examples of humanized mAbs include Daclizumab and trastuzumab. The culmination of all methodologies has led to present-day mAbs production capable of producing fully human mAbs and minimizing the risks originally associated with their predecessors. An example of mAbs generated by recombinant DNA methodologies includes adalimumab. The nomenclature of mAbs depends on the origin of each respective mAb. Common suffixes include -omab, -ximab, -zumab, and -umab, representing murine, chimeric, humanized, and human agents, respectively. [2] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: MAbs have evolved drastically since their inception in the 1970s by Drs Kohler and Milstein. The initial agents were murine in origin and presented several issues with their continued use, including immunogenicity and the potential for anaphylaxis with repeated exposure. As the research in immunology, molecular biology, and biochemistry continued to advance, it became possible to create chimeric, humanized, and, eventually, fully humanized mAbs.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Dosing in both clinical trials involved eight infusions over six months. However, the duration and total infusion numbers are debatable, as an improvement has been reported by injection 2 or 3. Smith et al. showed that the therapeutic effects were rapid, with 43% of patients in the teprotumumab group and 4% of patients in the placebo group responding at week 6. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Ocrelizumab is a second-generation anti-CD20 recombinant monoclonal antibody with a molecular mass of 145kDa. It is a follow-up to the anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody rituximab but with a humanized IgG1 tail. [3] Humanization of the antibody is expected to reduce antigenicity and improve CD20 binding. [4] CD20 is a phosphoprotein expressed on a broad range of human B-cells. However, it is not present in the early lineage (stem cells and pro-B-cells) or late lineage (plasmablasts and plasma cells). [5] Ocrelizumab binds to CD20 on B-lymphocytes leading to antibody-dependent cellular cytolysis and complement-mediated lysis. [6] While the precise method by which ocrelizumab exerts its effect on multiple sclerosis is unknown, it depletes B-cells which are thought to affect the pathogenesis of multiple sclerosis using cytokine regulation, antigen presentation, autoantibody production. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocrelizumab -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Ocrelizumab is an FDA-approved CD20-directed humanized monoclonal antibody indicated to treat adult patients with primary progressive or relapsing multiple sclerosis; it received approval from the FDA on March 28, 2017. retrieved_docs.title: Understanding How Monoclonal Antibodies Work -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: As evidenced by the classification system by Pichler et al. (2006), adverse events are still possible despite the advancements from murine antibodies to human/ humanized mAbs and the associated reduction in immunogenicity. Recombinant mAbs do not harbor the infectious concerns associated with human donor blood-type products, yet they are associated with their own unique risks as biologic products. This unique class of medications is also associated with its own subset of additional considerations during prescribing. For example, the use of TNF-a inhibitors such as Adalimumab warrants the additional step of ensuring that the patient does not have underlying latent tuberculosis (TB). The granulomas formed in TB infections contain the bacteria in a localized area and are kept by TNF-a signaling. Once inhibition of TNF-a occurs (for example, by administration of mAbs), the bacteria’s confinement is compromised, and reactivation of latent infection occurs. By the same token, biologics act by inhibiting the host's immune function and thereby lower the body’s defense mechanisms, potentially predisposing recipients to opportunistic infections, atypical infections (i.e., fungal), and sepsis. With these implications in mind, it is important for prescribers to be aware of and prepared to address adverse events that may occur with these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe disease who continue demonstrating response at 24 weeks to teprotumumab can be considered for additional infusions using the same dosing schedule. [56] In contrast, early discontinuation should be considered if there is no improvement at the 4th to 6th infusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 630
retrieved_docs.content: Tanezumab, abciximab and ibalizumab are all classified within the broader category of monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies). The relationship “isa” indicates that abciximab is a monoclonal antibody, while “mapped_to” shows that ibalizumab is linked to the monoclonal‑antibody class. The “inverse_isa” relationship denotes that monoklonální protilátky constitute the general class to which tanezumab belongs, confirming that tanezumab is a specific monoclonal antibody. Together, these connections highlight that each of these therapeutic agents shares the fundamental characteristic of being monoclonal antibodies, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action and clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: Abciximab and ibalizumab are both classified as monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies), indicating that each of these agents belongs to the therapeutic class of antibodies derived from a single immune cell clone and designed to target specific antigens. This shared categorization highlights their role in targeted medical treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1386
retrieved_docs.content: Abciximab is classified as a monoclonal antibody.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 478
retrieved_docs.content: CP 609754 is a more specific entity that falls under the broader category of technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab, indicating a narrower relationship to this radiolabeled antibody preparation. Technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab itself originates from both monoclonal antibodies (“monoklonální protilátky”) and organotechnetium compounds (“organotechneciové sloučeniny”), reflecting its dual derivation as a labeled biological agent. Within the class of monoclonal antibodies, abciximab is explicitly classified as a member, while ibalizumab is mapped to this same class, underscoring its identity as a monoclonal antibody therapeutic. Tanezumab represents a specific instance of the monoclonal antibody class, illustrating an inverse “is‑a” relationship where the broader category (monoclonal antibodies) encompasses this particular drug. Together, these connections highlight how diagnostic agents such as technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab are built from antibody and technetium chemistry, and how various therapeutic monoclonal antibodies—including abciximab, ibalizumab, and tanezumab—share a common biological framework that informs their clinical use and development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other antivirals in ATC” encompasses a diverse set of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtid, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron, all of which are classified within this ATC subgroup. In addition to its antiviral activity, ibalizumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, linking it to the biologic class “monoklonální protilátky.” Similarly, abciximab is explicitly described as a monoclonal antibody. This overlap illustrates that some agents within the “Other antivirals in ATC” group, such as ibalizumab, serve dual roles as both antiviral drugs and monoclonal antibody therapeutics, highlighting the convergence of antiviral pharmacotherapy and targeted biologic treatments in modern patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 103
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies (monoklonální protilátky) constitute a therapeutic class that includes tanezumab, abciximab, and ibalizumab. Tanezumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, abciximab is classified as a monoclonal antibody, and ibalizumab is mapped to the monoclonal antibody class. Within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system, the category “Other antivirals in ATC” comprises the antiviral agents enfuvirtid, maravirok, moroxydine, fostemsavir, and ibalizumab. Consequently, ibalizumab uniquely belongs to both the monoclonal antibody group and the “Other antivirals in ATC” group, highlighting an overlap between biologic immunotherapies and antiviral treatments. Recognizing these interconnections assists clinicians in understanding the dual therapeutic roles of certain agents and in navigating classification schemes for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1602
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is classified within the therapeutic class of Immunological Agents [TC] and is designated as an Immunomodulator (disposition). It is a specific type of Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of action that targets adhesion molecules to modulate immune cell interactions. Additionally, crizanlizumab is recognized as an imunosupresiva, indicating its broader role as an immunosuppressive agent. Together, these classifications emphasize crizanlizumab’s function as an immunological drug that modulates immune responses through selective inhibition of adhesion molecules, positioning it within both the immunomodulator and immunosuppressive categories of therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 91
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies (monoklonální protilátky) form a therapeutic class that includes abciximab and ibalizumab, and tanezumab is also classified as a monoclonal antibody. Ibalizumab is explicitly mapped to the monoclonal‑antibody class, reinforcing its identity as a biologic antibody agent. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system, the subgroup “Other antimycotics for systemic use” comprises the antifungal agents kaspofungin and micafungin. Both agents belong to the broader chemical family of lipopeptides; kaspofungin is recorded as having lipopeptides as its parent structure, and micafungin is listed as a member of the same group. The ATC category “Other antivirals” includes a diverse set of antiviral drugs: bulevirtide, enfuvirtid, maraviroc (maravirok), maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, and ibalizumab. Bulevirtide is linked to the lipopeptide class through a mapping that indicates it is derived from lipopeptides, creating a chemical‑structural connection between this antiviral and the lipopeptide‑based antifungals. Ibalizumab, while an antiviral, also belongs to the monoclonal‑antibody class, illustrating an overlap between antiviral therapy and biologic antibody treatments. Overall, the knowledge base highlights how several therapeutic agents share common classifications—monoclonal antibodies (abciximab, ibalizumab, tanezumab) and lipopeptide‑derived compounds (kaspofungin, micafungin, bulevirtide)—and how the ATC system groups antifungal and antiviral drugs, emphasizing both chemical relationships and functional categories that are relevant for drug selection and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 260
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use” comprises the antifungal agents kaspofungin and micafungin. Both agents are associated with the lipopeptidy chemical family; kaspofungin is explicitly noted to have lipopeptidy as its parent structure, indicating that micafungin likely shares this structural class as well. The ATC category “Other antivirals in ATC” includes a diverse set of antiviral drugs: bulevirtide, maravirok (maraviroc), maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, and tiloron. Bulevirtide is linked to the lipopeptidy class through a “mapped from” relationship, suggesting that its molecular scaffold is derived from or related to lipopeptidy compounds. Ibalizumab is simultaneously classified as a member of the antiviral ATC group and as a monoclonal antibody; it is explicitly “mapped to” the monoclonal antibody class, confirming its biologic nature. In addition, abciximab is identified as a monoclonal antibody (“isa” relationship), underscoring that monoclonal antibodies serve as a therapeutic modality across different indications, including antiviral therapy (ibalizumab) and antiplatelet therapy (abciximab). Together, these relationships illustrate how drugs are organized by therapeutic ATC categories, linked to underlying chemical families such as lipopeptidy, and further defined by their biologic modality when applicable, providing a comprehensive view of their classification and potential mechanisms of action for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: The group labeled “Other antivirals in ATC” encompasses a diverse set of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtid, maravirok, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron. Within this collection, ibalizumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, linking it to the broader class of monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies). Similarly, abciximab is classified as a monoclonal antibody. This illustrates that the “Other antivirals in ATC” category contains both small‑molecule entry inhibitors such as enfuvirtid, maravirok, and fostemsavir, as well as biologic therapies like ibalizumab and abciximab, highlighting the varied mechanisms and formats of antiviral treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 120
retrieved_docs.content: Monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies) encompass several therapeutic agents, including abciximab, tanezumab, and ibalizumab. The relationship “isa” indicates that abciximab is a monoclonal antibody, while the “inverse_isa” entry shows that tanezumab is also a monoclonal antibody. Ibalizumab is explicitly mapped to the monoclonal antibody class, confirming its identity as a monoclonal antibody as well. In addition to its classification as a monoclonal antibody, ibalizumab is listed as a member of the pharmacological group “Other antivirals in ATC.” This group comprises a range of antiviral compounds, specifically enfuvirtid, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, and tiloron. The “member_of” relationships indicate that each of these agents—enfuvirtid, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, and tiloron—is part of the “Other antivirals in ATC” category. Consequently, ibalizumab holds a dual classification: it functions both as a monoclonal antibody and as an antiviral agent within the ATC antiviral subgroup, highlighting the overlap between immunologic and antiviral therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies (monoklonální protilátky) comprise a therapeutic class that includes abciximab, ibalizumab and tanezumab. abciximab is explicitly classified as a monoclonal antibody, and ibalizumab is both mapped to and recognized as a monoclonal antibody; the inverse‑isa relationship also indicates that tanezumab belongs to the monoclonal‑antibody group. In addition to their immunologic mechanisms, certain monoclonal antibodies function as antiviral agents and are therefore grouped within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “Other antivirals in ATC.” This antiviral class contains enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, tiloron, and ibalizumab. The inclusion of ibalizumab in both categories reflects its dual role as a monoclonal antibody and as an antiviral medication (e.g., an entry inhibitor for HIV). Together, these relationships illustrate how specific biologic agents can be classified by both their molecular nature (monoclonal antibodies) and their therapeutic application (antiviral drugs), which is important for accurate drug categorization, prescribing, and patient management.
